schools.summary: Chrysostom: great and admirable is the dignity of an apostle, and Paul constantly sets forth the causes of it, not as if he took the honour to himself but as intrusted with it and under the necessity of so doing (as when he says he was 'called' and 'by the will of God,' and 'a necessity is laid upon me,' 1 Corinthians 9:16); Paul opening the letter as an apostle of Jesus Christ by the commandment of God our Saviour and the Lord Jesus Christ, who is our hope — the apostleship by divine command and the titles of God and Christ (Paul grounding his office not in his own will but in the express 'commandment' of God, his apostleship laid on him by divine appointment, and naming God 'our Saviour,' the author of salvation, and the Lord Jesus Christ 'our hope,' the ground and substance of the believer's hope, so the very salutation carries rich doctrine).

themes: Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the commandment of God our Saviour
Lord Jesus Christ, which is our hope
the apostleship by divine command
God our Saviour, Christ our hope

schools.summary: Gill: Timothy was Paul's own son in the faith — not in the flesh or by natural descent (as some have thought), but in a spiritual sense, in the faith of Christ, though the apostle was not properly his spiritual father or the instrument of his conversion (Timothy being already a converted person and a disciple of Christ); Paul addressing Timothy as his own son in the faith, with the benediction of grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Jesus Christ — the tender bond and the fuller blessing to a fellow-minister (Paul greeting Timothy as a true spiritual son, the affection of a father in the gospel, and invoking upon him not the usual 'grace and peace' only but adding 'mercy' between them, a fuller blessing fitting for a minister of the gospel who has special need of God's mercy in his great work, all flowing from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together).

themes: unto Timothy, my own son in the faith
grace, mercy, and peace
from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord
the added 'mercy' fitting for a minister

schools.summary: Barnes: it is clear from this that Paul and Timothy had been labouring together at Ephesus, and the language accords with the supposition that Paul had been compelled to leave before he had completed what he wished, leaving Timothy to carry it on; Paul reminding Timothy that he had besought him to remain at Ephesus, when Paul went into Macedonia, that he might charge some to teach no other doctrine — the commission to guard the church against false doctrine (Paul recalling the reason he had left Timothy at Ephesus: not for idleness but with a solemn charge to 'charge some,' the false teachers troubling the church, 'that they teach no other doctrine' than the apostolic gospel, so Timothy's first and chief task was the guarding of sound doctrine and the silencing of error).

themes: I besought thee to abide still at Ephesus
that thou mightest charge some
that they teach no other doctrine
Timothy's task the guarding of sound doctrine

schools.summary: Gill: the 'fables' are old wives' fables or Jewish fables, the traditions of the elders — anything not true, or, if true, yet idle, vain, trifling, and unprofitable; Barnes: they were probably the idle and puerile superstitions of the Jews; Paul charging that Timothy give no heed to fables and endless genealogies, which minister questions rather than godly edifying in faith — the rejection of vain speculation in favour of edifying faith (Paul specifying what the false teachers peddled: 'fables,' the unprofitable myths and traditions of the Jews, and 'endless genealogies,' interminable tracing of descents and speculations, which 'minister questions,' breed disputes and idle controversies, rather than the true 'godly edifying which is in faith,' the building up of souls in the faith, so Timothy is to turn from barren speculation to what truly edifies).

themes: neither give heed to fables and endless genealogies
which minister questions
rather than godly edifying which is in faith
turning from speculation to what edifies

schools.summary: Chrysostom: nothing is so injurious to mankind as to undervalue friendship and love and not to cultivate it with the greatest care, as nothing is so beneficial as to pursue it to the utmost (Christ showing this, 'if two of you shall agree on earth...'); Paul, against the wranglers, sets up love as the very end and scope of all the commandment; Paul declaring that the end of the commandment is charity out of a pure heart, a good conscience, and faith unfeigned — the goal of all teaching is love (Paul setting against the false teachers' barren wrangling the true 'end,' aim, design, and scope, of the whole commandment and gospel charge: not vain disputes but 'charity,' love to God and man, flowing from three holy springs: 'a pure heart,' cleansed and renewed, 'a good conscience,' free from guilt and hypocrisy, and 'faith unfeigned,' a real, sincere faith, so the whole of true religion comes to flower in love born of a cleansed and believing heart).

themes: now the end of the commandment is charity
out of a pure heart
of a good conscience, and of faith unfeigned
the goal of all teaching is love

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle describes the persons he suspected of teaching other doctrines and introducing fables and endless genealogies — such who departed from the above things (love, a pure heart, a good conscience, and unfeigned faith); they erred from the commandment or law, notwithstanding their great pretensions to a regard for it, or at least missed the mark, the end and design of it; Paul declaring that from these some, having swerved, have turned aside unto vain jangling — the departure from the true aim into empty wrangling (Paul showing the sad result when the false teachers missed the true 'end' of the commandment: having 'swerved,' missed the mark and departed from love and a pure heart and sincere faith, they 'turned aside unto vain jangling,' empty, idle, and unprofitable talk, mere noise and dispute, so that, abandoning the goal of love, they fell into fruitless babbling).

themes: from which some having swerved
have turned aside unto vain jangling
the departure from love into empty wrangling
abandoning the goal, falling into babbling

schools.summary: Gill: they were very fond of being called Rabbi, Rabbi, and styled doctors of the law, and of being thought to have skill in interpreting the law and good talents in expounding and preaching on it, which was now most in vogue and gained the greatest applause, when the preaching of the gospel was treated with contempt not only by the unbelieving Jews but by judaizing teachers; Paul declaring that they desire to be teachers of the law, understanding neither what they say nor whereof they affirm — the ambition of ignorant men to be law-teachers (Paul exposing the false teachers' real character: filled with the ambition 'to be teachers of the law,' craving the honour and title of doctors of the law then in high vogue, yet utterly incompetent, 'understanding neither what they say,' not even grasping their own assertions, 'nor whereof they affirm,' nor the subjects they so confidently pronounced upon, empty pretenders ignorant of the very thing they professed to teach).

themes: desiring to be teachers of the law
understanding neither what they say
nor whereof they affirm
the ambition of ignorant men to teach

schools.summary: Gill: Paul says this to prevent an objection that might be made — that seeing he bore so hard on those fond of being teachers of the law, he was himself against the law and the preaching and proper use of it; but this is not to be concluded, for he and his fellow labourers and all true believers know from the Scriptures of truth that the law is good; Paul affirming that the law is good if a man use it lawfully — the goodness of the law in its right use (Paul, having rebuked the false law-teachers, guarding against any charge that he despised the law itself: 'we know that the law is good,' holy, just, and good in its own nature as God's own, yet only 'if a man use it lawfully,' in its proper intended way, not as a means of justification which it cannot give but for the ends God designed, so the fault lay not in the law but in its abuse by the ignorant teachers).

themes: but we know that the law is good
if a man use it lawfully
the goodness of the law in its right use
the fault in its abuse, not the law

schools.summary: Gill: no man is naturally righteous since Adam (excepting the man Christ Jesus); some righteous in their own opinion or others' esteem are not truly so; none are righteous or can be justified before God by the works of the law; those only are righteous men who are made so through the imputation of Christ's righteousness, and for such the law is not made as a terror; Paul declaring that the law is not made for a righteous man but for the lawless, disobedient, ungodly, sinners, unholy, profane, and murderers — the law's restraining terror aimed at the wicked not the justified (Paul explaining the lawful use of the law: 'not made for a righteous man,' the believer justified by Christ's righteousness, led by the Spirit and needing no lash of threat, but 'for the lawless and disobedient,' naming a fearful catalogue of the grossly wicked, the ungodly, profane, parricides, murderers, against whom the law stands as a curb, a terror, and a condemnation, restraining and exposing their sin).

sides.position: Not for the righteous (the believer justified by Christ, led by the Spirit, who needs no lash of threat) but for the lawless and wicked, against whom it stands as a curb, terror, and condemnation

themes: knowing this, that the law is not made for a righteous man
but for the lawless and disobedient
for the ungodly and for sinners... murderers
the law's terror for the wicked, not the justified

schools.summary: Gill: whoremongers (fornicators and adulterers, transgressors of the seventh command) God will judge, and such shall have their portion in the lake of fire; Barnes: 'them that defile themselves with mankind' are sodomites (the crime abounding in ancient times, Romans 1:27); Paul continuing the list — whoremongers, sodomites, menstealers (slave-traders), liars, perjured persons — and all that is contrary to sound doctrine — the further sins the law condemns and the test of sound teaching (Paul completing his catalogue of the gross sins against which the law is directed, sexual immorality of various kinds, the man-stealing of slave-traders, lying, and perjury, and then, with a sweeping clause, adding 'and if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine,' so the standard of judgment is the 'sound doctrine' of the gospel, and whatever opposes that wholesome teaching is condemned along with the rest).

themes: for whoremongers... menstealers... liars... perjured persons
if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine
the catalogue of gross sins the law condemns
sound doctrine the standard of judgment

schools.summary: Gill: no doctrine is sound but what is agreeable to the gospel — a very great encomium of it: the doctrine preached by the apostles was not only gospel, or good news and glad tidings, but the gospel of God, of which he is the author, which relates to his glory, the glory of all his perfections, which reveals his purposes, shows his covenant, and exhibits the blessings; Paul declaring that this sound doctrine is according to the glorious gospel of the blessed God, committed to his trust — the sound doctrine measured by the glorious gospel entrusted to Paul (Paul naming the standard by which all teaching is judged: 'the glorious gospel of the blessed God,' called 'glorious' because it displays all God's perfections and glory, and 'of the blessed God' as its author, and adding that this gospel 'was committed to my trust,' so he holds it as a sacred deposit, a stewardship to be guarded and faithfully dispensed).

themes: according to the glorious gospel of the blessed God
which was committed to my trust
sound doctrine measured by the glorious gospel
a sacred deposit to be guarded and dispensed

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the advantages of humility are generally acknowledged, yet it is a thing not easily met with — there is affectation of humble talking enough and to spare, but humbleness of mind nowhere; this quality Paul so cultivated that he is ever looking out for inducements to be humble, and those conscious of great merits must struggle much with themselves to be humble; Paul thanking Christ Jesus our Lord, who enabled him, counting him faithful and putting him into the ministry — the grateful acknowledgment of Christ's enabling grace and trust (Paul, about to recall his own former sin, first breaking into thanksgiving to Christ for three things: that he 'hath enabled me,' given strength and gifts for the work, that 'he counted me faithful,' judged and made him trustworthy, and that he was 'putting me into the ministry,' placing so great a trust in his hands, so Paul owns his whole ministry, its power, trust, and very calling, as the gracious gift of Christ).

themes: I thank Christ Jesus our Lord, who hath enabled me
for that he counted me faithful
putting me into the ministry
his whole ministry the gift of Christ's grace

schools.summary: Gill: Paul was before a blasphemer of the name of Christ, contrary to which he thought he ought to do many things, calling him an impostor and deceiver, and not only blaspheming that name himself but compelling others to blaspheme it also; this and what follows is said to illustrate the grace of God in his conversion and call to the ministry; Paul confessing that he was before a blasphemer, persecutor, and injurious, but obtained mercy because he did it ignorantly in unbelief — the chief of sinners obtaining mercy in his ignorance (Paul, to magnify the grace just owned, recalling his dark past: a 'blasphemer' reviling Christ and his name, a 'persecutor' violently hunting the church, and 'injurious,' insolent and outrageous in his hostility, yet 'I obtained mercy,' and adding the ground on which God dealt mercifully: 'because I did it ignorantly in unbelief,' not in the malice of a known truth resisted but in the blindness of unbelief, so his sin, though great, was not the unpardonable sin against light).

themes: who was before a blasphemer, and a persecutor, and injurious
but I obtained mercy
because I did it ignorantly in unbelief
mercy to the former persecutor, magnifying grace

schools.summary: Gill: the grace here is the love of God (the Ethiopic reading 'the grace of God,' of God the Father, distinguished in the text from Jesus Christ); God is abundant in grace and goodness, rich and plenteous in mercy, and there is an overflow of love in his heart to his chosen people, which in conversion flows out and abounds and superabounds; Paul declaring that the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant, with faith and love in Christ Jesus — the superabounding grace that brought faith and love where sin had reigned (Paul crowning his confession by magnifying the grace that met his great sin: 'the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant,' not merely abundant but overflowing and superabounding, more than equal to his guilt, and that grace came 'with faith and love which is in Christ Jesus,' so that where blasphemy and hatred had ruled, grace planted the very opposite graces, the faith that believes in Christ and the love that cleaves to him, both flowing from him as their source).

themes: and the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant
with faith and love which is in Christ Jesus
superabounding grace over Paul's sin
faith and love planted where hatred ruled

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the favours of God so far exceed human hope and expectation that often they are not believed, for God has bestowed such things as the mind of man never looked for; so the apostles spend much discourse in securing a belief of the gifts granted us of God, as men receiving some great good ask themselves if it is not a dream, not believing it; Paul declaring it a faithful saying, worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom he is chief — the heart of the gospel sealed and Paul's own place as chief of sinners (Paul gathering the whole gospel into one weighty, trustworthy maxim, 'Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners,' sealing it as 'a faithful saying,' certainly true, and 'worthy of all acceptation,' to be embraced by all, so the purpose of Christ's coming was the saving of sinners as such, and Paul, far from excepting himself, claiming the lowest place, 'of whom I am chief,' counting himself the foremost of sinners saved, so his own case becomes the great proof of the saying's truth).

themes: this is a faithful saying, and worthy of all acceptation
Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners
of whom I am chief
the gospel's heart sealed, Paul the chief example

schools.summary: Barnes: this was one of the causes, a leading reason, of his pardon — not the only one, for God had other ends; but this was a design of his conversion, that there might be for all ages a permanent proof that sins of the deepest dye may be forgiven; Paul declaring that for this cause he obtained mercy, that in him first Jesus might show all longsuffering as a pattern for those who should hereafter believe — the pardon of the chief sinner set as a permanent pattern of hope (Paul, having owned himself chief of sinners, seeing in his own conversion a divine design: God obtained mercy for him 'first,' as a foremost or pre-eminent instance, 'that in me Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering,' displaying the full extent of his patient mercy on the worst of men, 'for a pattern,' a sample and encouraging example, to all who should 'hereafter believe on him to life everlasting,' so no sinner, however great, need despair, the chief of sinners being saved to assure the rest).

themes: for this cause I obtained mercy
that in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering
for a pattern to them which should hereafter believe
the chief sinner's pardon a pattern of hope

schools.summary: Gill: this doxology, or ascription of glory to God on account of the grace bestowed upon the apostle, may be considered as referring to all three divine Persons, Father, Son, and Spirit, who are the one and only God, to whom all the attributes of wisdom, power, eternity, immortality (incorruptibleness), and invisibility belong, and who are jointly concerned in the grace bestowed upon any; Paul ascribing honour and glory for ever to the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God — the outburst of praise to the glorious God on account of his grace (Paul, overwhelmed by the grace shown to him, breaking into a great doxology, heaping up the incommunicable titles of God: 'King eternal,' the everlasting Sovereign of all ages, 'immortal,' incorruptible and deathless, 'invisible,' unseen by mortal eyes, 'the only wise God,' alone possessing perfect wisdom, and ascribing to this God 'honour and glory for ever and ever,' sealing it with 'Amen,' so the contemplation of grace rises into the worship of the glorious Giver).

themes: now unto the King eternal, immortal, invisible
the only wise God
be honour and glory for ever and ever. Amen
the contemplation of grace rising into worship

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the office of a teacher and that of a priest is of great dignity, and to bring forward one that is worthy requires a divine election; so it was of old and so it is now, when we make a choice without human passion, not looking to any temporal consideration, swayed neither by friendship nor enmity (for though we be not partakers of so great a measure of the Spirit as they, a good purpose is sufficient to draw the election); Paul committing this charge to Timothy, according to the prophecies that went before on him, that he might war a good warfare — the ministry as a warfare entered by divine designation (Paul, returning to his main purpose, solemnly 'committing,' delivering as a sacred trust, 'this charge' to Timothy his son, and reminding him that his calling was marked out 'according to the prophecies which went before on thee,' the inspired utterances that had pointed him out for the work, so that, strengthened by the memory that God himself had designated him, Timothy might 'war a good warfare,' fighting the noble fight of faith against error and for the truth, as a soldier of Christ).

themes: this charge I commit unto thee, son Timothy
according to the prophecies which went before on thee
that thou by them mightest war a good warfare
the ministry a warfare entered by divine designation

schools.summary: Gill: by 'faith' is meant not the grace of faith but the doctrine of faith (a sense often used in this epistle), and the 'holding' of it is not a mere profession or retaining without wavering (which all believers do), but a holding it forth in the ministry of the word, in opposition to concealing or dropping it or any part of it; joined to a good conscience, which keeps the doctrine pure; Paul charging Timothy to hold faith and a good conscience, which some having put away have made shipwreck of faith — the bond of sound doctrine and a good conscience, and the ruin of those who part them (Paul naming the twin things Timothy must keep in his warfare: 'faith,' the sound doctrine of the gospel held fast and held forth, and 'a good conscience,' purity and integrity of heart and life, the two closely bound, for a corrupt conscience soon corrupts the doctrine, and warning by the example of those who, 'having put away' a good conscience, have 'made shipwreck' of the faith itself, the loss of holiness leading to the wreck of belief).

themes: holding faith, and a good conscience
which some having put away
concerning faith have made shipwreck
the loss of holiness wrecking belief

schools.summary: Gill: Hymenaeus (mentioned in 2 Timothy 2:17) made shipwreck of the doctrine of the resurrection of the dead, whereby the faith of some nominal believers was overthrown, attended with the putting away of a good conscience he seemingly before had, his profane and vain babblings increasing to more ungodliness; Alexander seems to be the same coppersmith who did Paul much evil (2 Timothy 4:14); Paul naming Hymenaeus and Alexander, whom he had delivered to Satan that they might learn not to blaspheme — the apostolic discipline of two notorious errorists for their correction (Paul giving two examples of those who had wrecked their faith, Hymenaeus who denied or perverted the resurrection and Alexander likely the coppersmith who opposed Paul, whom Paul had 'delivered unto Satan,' by his apostolic authority excommunicating them and giving them over to Satan's buffeting, perhaps with bodily affliction, and that not for their destruction but their correction, 'that they may learn not to blaspheme,' the severe act aimed at bringing them to repentance).

debates.question: What is the purpose of delivering Hymenaeus and Alexander to Satan?

sides.position: Not their destruction but their correction — the apostolic excommunication and giving over to Satan's buffeting aimed at bringing them to repentance, 'that they may learn not to blaspheme'

themes: of whom is Hymenaeus and Alexander
whom I have delivered unto Satan
that they may learn not to blaspheme
apostolic discipline aimed at correction
